item.title: 使用 GitHub 的项目工具规划和跟踪项目 | Planning And Tracking Projects With GitHub's Projects Tool
item.description:
各位 Codenewbies 👋，

前阵子写了一篇文章，叫《如何使用GitHub进行项目协作——基于敏捷方法》 。

从那时起，GitHub 的项目板有了新的更新。 所以我写这篇文章是对我上一篇文章中“创建项目板”部分的更新。

什么是项目？

这些项目与看板具有相同的目的。 如果您从未听说过，看板是敏捷环境中一种常见且广泛使用的框架，用于查看项目工作的状态。

即使我们正在处理一个单独的项目，我们仍然可以利用 GitHub 上的项目板来让我们按计划和执行我们的项目。
该项目板与我们的问题和拉取请求集成在一起。 因此，我们可以轻松地将我们在董事会上的计划转化为问题，并跟踪我们的问题和拉取请求的进度。

本文将介绍项目工具的最基本用法，适用于独立项目或小型团队。

现在，让我们创建一个项目板并开始动手吧！ 使用 GitHub 上的项目板

首先，转到您的个人资料，然后单击导航栏右侧的头像。 然后，选择Your projects选项。

然后，在顶部栏上，单击“ Projects ”选项卡。

创建一个新的项目板

要创建新的项目板，请单击New project的绿色按钮。

系统将提示您选择一个带有几个选项的模板； 要学习本教程，您需要Start from scratch 。 您可以为电子表格视图布局选择Table ，或为简单的任务Board选择板。 您选择什么并不重要，因为您可以随时切换到任何格式。 但是现在，继续选择Board布局，然后单击绿色的Create按钮。

现在您有一个项目板，其中包含Todo 、 In progress和Done列。

添加新列

您可能想要对您的问题（或任何其他任务）进行一些代码审查，并且需要对其进行跟踪。 因此，让我们为其创建一个新列。

  • 单击列外部的+按钮。

  • 添加标题并单击输入。

如有必要，您可以通过拖动来更改列的顺序。

公开项目板

默认情况下，新创建的项目板是私有的。 当你做一个单独的项目时会很好。 但是当您在团队中工作时，您希望将可见性更改为公开。

  • 在项目板中，单击右上角的三点按钮并选择Settings 。

  • 转到Danger zone部分中的Visibility 。 单击“ Private按钮，然后从下拉列表中选择Public ”。

关闭项目将从打开的项目列表中删除该项目。 删除项目将一劳永逸地删除您的项目。

计划一个项目

准备好项目板后，就可以开始规划项目了。
让我们从更改顶部的项目标题开始。

现在您可以添加任务。 有两种方法可以做到这一点。 我将带您了解它们。

  1. 使用存储库中的现有问题

假设您有一个存储库，其中包含要添加到项目板的现有问题。 让我们将这个问题添加到Todo列。

  • 单击Todo列底部的+ Add item按钮。

  • 键入 # 并选择存储库。

    您将看到存在问题的存储库列表。 选择目标存储库，然后选择问题。

    你有了第一个基于你的问题的待办事项！

如果您没有要添加到项目板的问题，您可以写一个新的问题草稿。

单击Todo 、 In progress或任何其他列底部的+ Add item按钮，具体取决于您希望此草稿所在的位置。

然后为草稿问题添加标题并按回车键。

现在让我们为草稿添加描述，就像您为问题添加描述一样。

  • 单击草稿的标题。

  • 添加描述、编辑标题、添加受让人和更改状态。

    您可以通过单击“ Edit ”按钮添加描述和编辑标题。 您还可以添加受让人并更改进度状态。

  • 将草稿转化为问题。

    在为草稿添加描述后，您还可以选择将草稿转换为问题。

    但如果您需要更多时间来添加描述，请将鼠标悬停在草稿问题卡上。 然后单击草稿文本旁边的三点图标，然后单击Convert to issue 。

    现在搜索您希望此问题所在的存储库并单击它。

    瞧！ 现在您的项目存储库中有一个新问题。

删除项目板上的问题只会将其从项目板上删除，而不会从存储库中删除。

更新进度状态并跟踪项目

默认情况下，首次创建项目板时有三个进度状态列。 它们是Todo 、 In progress和Done列。

每当您想更改其进度状态时，您可以将问题或草稿问题卡从一列拖到另一列。

如果您已经发出拉取请求并在拉取请求中链接了问题，您可以在项目板上的问题卡中看到拉取请求的链接。 单击问题的标题。 在右侧栏中，单击Show all fields 。

当您的问题关闭时，问题卡会自动从原来的列移动到Done列。

从问题页面检查和更改项目板上问题的进度状态

假设您想跟踪或更新项目板上问题的进度状态。 你需要知道你的问题属于哪个项目，因为你有很多项目。

在问题页面的右侧，查找Projects 。 项目的名称和任务的进度状态位于此部分中。
假设您要更改进度状态。 例如，从Todo到In progress 。 单击Status下拉菜单并选择In progress 。 项目板上的问题卡将移至您选择的列。
如果要打开项目板，请单击项目名称。

最后的话

项目板是个人或团队项目组织任务的绝佳工具。 现在，您可以开始使用 GitHub 上的项目板计划和跟踪项目进度！ 感谢您的阅读！
最后，您可以在Twitter和Mastodon上找到我。 让我们联系起来！ 😊 😊

📸 Cover image by Glenn Carstens-Peters on Unsplash 📸 Unsplash上的Glenn Carstens-Peters的封面图片



原文:

Hello Fellow Codenewbies 👋,

A while ago, I wrote an article called How To Use GitHub For Project Collaboration — Based on Agile Method.

Since then, GitHub has had new updates for the projects board. So I'm writing this article as an update of the section "Create project board" within my previous article.

What is Projects?

Projects is an adaptable, flexible tool for planning and tracking work on GitHub.
— GitHub docs

The Projects has the same purpose as a Kanban board. If you have never heard about it, Kanban is a common and widely used framework in an Agile environment to see the state of the project's work.

Even when we're working on a solo project, we can still take advantage of the project board on GitHub to keep us on track with planning and executing our project.
This projects board is integrated with our issues and pull requests. So, we can easily convert our plan on the board into an issue, and track the progress of our issues and pull requests.

This article will cover the most basic use of the Projects tool, which is suitable for working on solo projects or with a small team.

Now, let's create a project board and get those hands wet! 😄

Working with the project board on GitHub

First, go to your profile and click on your avatar at the right side of the navbar. Then, select the Your projects option.

✍️ Note: If you can't find the Your projects option, select Your profile or Your repositories after you click on your avatar. Then, on the top bar, click the Projects tab.

Create a new project board

To create a new project board, click the green button of New project.

You will get prompted to select a template with a couple of options; Start from scratch and Project templates.
To follow this tutorial, you want to go with Start from scratch. You can choose Table for a spreadsheet view layout or Board for a simple task board. What you choose doesn't matter because you can always switch to any format. But for now, go ahead and select the Board layout, and click the green Create button.

Now you have a project board with Todo, In progress, and Done columns.

Add a new column

You may want to do some code reviews for your issues (or any other tasks) and need to track it. So let's create a new column for it.

  • Click the + button at the outer of the columns.

  • Add a title and click enter.

You can change the order of the columns by dragging them if necessary.

Make the project board public

By default, newly created project boards are private. It would be fine when you're doing a solo project. But when you're working in a team, you want to change the visibility to public.

  • In the project board, click the three dots button at the top right side and select Settings.

  • Go to Visibility in the Danger zone section. Click the Private button, and select Public from the dropdown.

✍️ Note: In Settings, there are also options to close and delete the project. Closing the project will remove the project from the list of open projects. And deleting the project will remove your project once and for all.

Planning a project

After you have your project board ready, you can start planning the project.
Let's start by changing the project title you can find at the top.

Now you can add the tasks. There are two ways of doing this. And I will walk you through them both.

  1. Use an existing issue in a repository

Say you have a repository with an existing issue you want to add to the project board. Let's add this issue to the Todo column.

  • Click the + Add item button at the bottom of the Todo column.

  • Type # and select the repository.

    You will see a list of repositories that have your issues. Select the target repository, then the issue.

    And you have your first todo item based on your issue!

You can write a new draft issue if you don't have an issue to be added to the project board.

Click the + Add item button at the bottom of the Todo, In progress, or any other column, depending on where you want this draft to be.

Then add a title for the draft issue and press enter.

Now let's add a description to the draft, just like you're adding a description for an issue.

  • Click the title of the draft.

  • Add a description, edit the title, add assignees, and change the status.

    You can add a description and edit the title by clicking the Edit button. You can also add the assignees and change the status of the progress.

  • Convert the draft into an issue.

    After you add a description to your draft, you can also opt-in to convert the draft into an issue.

    But if you need more time to add a description, hover over the draft issue card. Then click the three dots icon beside the draft text, and click Convert to issue.

    Now search the repository where you want this issue to be and click it.

    And voila! Now you have a new issue in your project's repository.

✍️ Note: When you click on the three dots, there are also options to archive and delete an issue or a draft issue. Deleting the issue on the project board will only remove it from the project board, not the repository.

Updating the progress status and tracking the project

By default, there are three progress status columns when you first create the project board. They are the Todo, In progress, and Done columns.

You can drag an issue or a draft issue card from one column to another whenever you want to change their progress status.

If you already made a pull request and linked the issue in your pull request, you can see the link to the pull request in the issue card on the project board. Click the issue's title. And on the right bar, click Show all fields.

And when your issue is closed, the issue card will automatically move from wherever column it was to the Done column.

Check and change the issue's progress status on the project board from the issue page

Say that you want to track or update the progress status of an issue on the project board. And you need to know which project your issue belongs to because you have many projects.

On the issue page, on the right side, look for Projects. The project's name and the task's progress status are in this section.
Suppose you want to change the progress status. For example, from Todo to In progress. Click the Status dropdown menu and select In progress. The issue card on the project board will then move to the column you've chosen.
Click the project name if you want to open the project board.

Final words

The projects board is a great tool for solo or team projects to organize your tasks. And now, you can start to plan and track the progress of your project with the projects board on GitHub! 🙌

Thank you for reading!
Last, you can find me on Twitter and Mastodon. Let's connect! 😊

📸 Cover image by Glenn Carstens-Peters on Unsplash
item.author: adiatiayu

item.title: 动态创建数组 | Dynamically Create an of Arrays
item.description:
永远微笑

发表于在最近的算法挑战中，我想创建一个包含十个“桶”的可迭代集合来对数据进行分组。 实际上，这意味着创建一个数组数组，例如：

 [[], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], []]

什么没有奏效

我不想对每组数组进行硬编码，而是想动态创建它。 我的第一次尝试（根据记忆和 MDN 文档的粗略浏览）是使用 Array 构造函数和链接的.fill方法：

 const arr = new Array(10).fill([]); console.log(arr); // => [[], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], []];

乍一看，这似乎行得通。 控制台记录我的新arr显示一个包含 10 个空数组的数组，所以我继续解决算法。

很快，当我开始填充内部数组（桶）时，我注意到有一个问题——将一个元素添加到一个“桶”，例如在索引 5 处，更新每个桶以匹配，而不是仅更新预期的桶:

 arr[5].push("hello"); console.log(arr); // => [["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"]];

返回Array.prototype.fill的 MDN 文档，问题变得很清楚：

由于数组是一种对象，我的每个数组桶实际上都在引用内存中的同一个对象。 因此，我的数组并没有创建 10 个不同的数组，而是仅显示同一数组的 10 个副本。

要动态创建数组数组（或其他对象），您可以改用.from方法：

 const newArr = Array.from({ length: 10 }, () => []); console.log(newArr); // => [[], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], []];

这里{length: 10}是“arrayLike”。 由于 duck-typing，length 属性可用（请参阅此处的 Stack Overflow 解释）。 现在，将一个元素推送到索引 5 处的数组，按预期仅更新该数组：

 newArr[5].push("hello"); console.log(newArr); // => [[], [], [], [], [], ["hello"], [], [], [], []];

约翰内斯·凯特曼 - 一旦暂停，smilesforgood 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

解除暂停后，smilesforgood 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，smilesforgood 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 smilesforgood 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区 👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 以下是标记 smilesforgood 的方法：

报告其他不当行为

坚定的 smilesforgood 将恢复他们帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

smilesforgood

Posted on Nov 27

In a recent algorithm challenge, I wanted to create an iterable collection of ten "buckets" for grouping data. In practice, this meant creating an array of arrays like:

[[], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], []]

What Did Not Work

Rather than hard code each set of arrays, I wanted to create this dynamically. My first attempt (from memory and a cursory skim of the MDN docs), was to use the Array constructor with the .fill method chained on:

const arr = new Array(10).fill([]);
console.log(arr);
// => [[], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], []];

At first glance, this seemed to work. Console logging my new arr revealed an array containing 10 empty arrays, so I continued solving the algorithm.

As soon, as I began populating the inner arrays (the buckets), I noticed that there was an issue - adding an element to one "bucket", for example at index 5, updated EVERY bucket to match, rather than only the intended bucket:

arr[5].push("hello");
console.log(arr);
// => [["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"], ["hello"]];

Returning to the MDN docs for Array.prototype.fill, the issue became clear:

As arrays are a type of object, each of my array buckets were actually referencing the same object in memory. So rather than creating 10 distinct arrays, my array was merely displaying 10 copies of the same array.

Array.from to the Rescue

To dynamically create an array of arrays (or other objects), you can instead use the .from method:

const newArr = Array.from({ length: 10 }, () => []);
console.log(newArr);
// => [[], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], []];

Here {length: 10} is the "arrayLike". The length property is available because of duck-typing (see a great Stack Overflow explanation here). () => [] is the "mapFn", whose return value is used to populate the array.

Now, pushing an element to the array at index 5, updates only that array, as expected:

newArr[5].push("hello");
console.log(newArr);
// => [[], [], [], [], [], ["hello"], [], [], [], []];

Johannes Kettmann - Nov 11

Once suspended, smilesforgood will not be able to comment or publish posts until their suspension is removed.

Once unsuspended, smilesforgood will be able to comment and publish posts again.

Once unpublished, all posts by smilesforgood will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

If smilesforgood is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

Thanks for keeping DEV Community 👩‍💻👨‍💻 safe. Here is what you can do to flag smilesforgood:

Report other inappropriate conduct

Unflagging smilesforgood will restore default visibility to their posts.

item.title: 版本 0.2 - PR #2 | Release 0.2 - PR #2
item.description:
发表于该网站支持“浅色”主题和“深色”主题以提高可读性。 我处理了第 69 期问题，其中用于选择主题的下拉菜单与教程列表重叠，导致一些标题难以阅读。

准备修复

应用修复不需要太多设置。 分叉回购协议后，我开始着手解决问题。

代码说明

如前所述，出于可读性目的，该网站支持“浅色”和“深色”主题。 可以通过“主题框”在这两个主题之间切换，这是一个用作下拉菜单的<div>元素。 在网站的主页上，有一个列表，列出了网站上当前托管的所有教程，这些教程被组织成一个表格。

在我进行更改之前，主题框会“漂浮”在网站的主要区域内，与表格最左侧的教程重叠。 我发现这看起来很不愉快，浏览时也有点不方便，所以我选择解决这个问题。

我的代码更改将主题框移动到标题的一部分，而不是它“漂浮”在教程列表前面的原始位置。

代码演示

下面我附上了网站主页的两个屏幕截图。 请注意主题框的位置，它已从浮动在教程列表上移至标题。

修复前

修复后

研究

  • 你做了什么研究？ 您是否发现问题的某些方面很困难？

我对这个项目中使用的 Bootstrap 类进行了一些个人研究，结果发现它们是我已经熟悉的结构，因此解决这个问题所需的准备工作比我最初预期的要少。

然而，我在更新代码以应用我的更改时遇到的一个问题是，如果通过元素上的include-html属性将一段 HTML 代码加载到文件中，那么app.js中定义的 JS 代码将不会适用于它。

因此，我不得不对我的初始修复（更新header.html文件的地方）进行一些小的更改，并将这些更改移至主index.html文件。

与项目维护者的互动

我与项目维护者没有明显的互动。

困难

我最大的困难是理解项目的代码，因为它相当混乱。 有很多不推荐使用的代码部分只是被注释掉了，而不是被删除和记录。 因此，在整个无错误代码中都放置了注释掉的错误代码。

找到需要更新的相关部分后，应用我的修复程序就不是很困难了。

拉取请求链接

公关 #71

一旦暂停，aparris21 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

解除暂停后，aparris21 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，aparris21 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 aparris21 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区 👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 以下是标记 aparris21 的方法：

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 aparris21 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

a-parris21

Posted on Nov 27

The html.org.in project is a website which has several simple tutorials aimed at teaching new web developers the basics of HTML.

The website supports "light" theme and "dark" theme for readibility. I worked on issue #69, where the drop-down menu for choosing the theme was overlapping with list of tutorials, making it difficult to read some of the titles.

Preparation for the fix

There was not much setup required to apply the fix. After forking the repo, I got to work on fixing the problem.

Code Explanation

As stated, the website supports "light" and "dark" themes for readibility purposes. CHanging between these two themes can be done via the "theme-box", which is a <div> element that functions as a drop-down menu. On the website's home page, there is a list of all the tutorials currently hosted on the website, organised into a table.

Before my changes, the theme-box would "float" within the website's main area, overlapping with the tutorials on the far-left of the table. I found this unpleasant to look at and mildly inconvenient when browsing so I opted to fix the problem.

My code changes move the theme-box to be part of the header, instead of its original position where it "floats" in-front of the list of tutorials.

Code Demo

Below I've attached two screenshots of the website's home page. Take note of the position theme-box, which is moved from floating over the tutorial list, to the header.

Before the fix

After the fix

Research

  • What research did you do? Were there aspects of the issue that you found difficult?

I did some personal research into the Bootstrap classes used in this project and they turned out to be structures I am already familiar with, so resolving this issue turned out to require less preparation than I initially expected.

However, a problem I ran into while updating the code to apply my changes was that, if a section of HTML code was loaded into a file via the include-html attribute on an element, then the JS code defined in app.js would not apply to it.

As such, I had to make some small changes to my initial fix (where I updated the header.html file) and move those changes to the main index.html file.

Interactions with project maintainers

I had no notable interactions with the project maintainers.

Difficulties

My greatest difficulty was in understanding the code of the project as it was fairly messy. There are a lot of deprecated sections of code that have just been commented out, rather than deleted and documented. Thus, there's commented-out buggy code placed througout bug-free code.

Once I found the relevant sections that I needed to update, it was not very difficult to apply my fixes.

Link to Pull Request

PR #71

item.title: 将 navi 用于 CLI 秘籍 | Using navi for CLI Cheats
item.description:
深入研究navi作为交互式命令生成器，并使用strace作为简明备忘单的演示！

在上一篇文章中，我花了很短的时间详细介绍了我为使命令行体验更加愉快和高效而采取的步骤。 我列出的步骤之一是使用工具navi快速调用
命令。

这对于我不经常使用的命令，或者对于我不常使用的具有特定选项的命令变体非常有用。 我发现通过我的navi作弊进行模糊搜索比通过grep ping 快 100 倍
联机帮助页或--help输出。 现在，当然有时我的navi作弊没有涵盖我需要的东西，我必须回到旧方法，但后来我只是更新我的作弊并开始我的一天。

我最近发现了一种新的（对我而言）使用navi的方式，作为各种“命令生成器”。 在标志/选项是附加的命令上使用是有意义的，而不是单独的命令（这有点让我讨厌......）

我应该在 CLI UX 上发一篇关于为什么某些 CLI 模式不是最佳的帖子......

输入strace

一个很好的例子是strace ，它允许显示由特定命令和进程进行的系统调用。 其他几篇博客文章最近提到了strace ，它是我手头上的一个工具，不仅仅是一个
好几次了，很荣幸这将是今天的演示。

我们不会添加strace拥有的所有选项，但会添加我发现最常见于我的用例的选项，这可能也涵盖了相当多的人。

我们的目标是构建一个navi备忘单，引导我们构建一个带有我们想要公开的所有选项的strace命令。

这篇文章不会涵盖strace的所有细节，甚至不会涵盖所有选项的含义，尽管我们添加的大多数选项都是不言自明的。

一个好的 CLI 应该是这样的！

有关很好的总结，请参阅“ strace The Sysadmins
 显微镜” 。

正如我在上一篇文章中提到的，我在我的终端中将navi绑定到<CTRL>-n ，在我的~/.zshrc .zshrc 中使用以下 ZSH 函数：

 _call_navi () { local selected if selected = " $( printf " $( navi --print --path ${ HOME } /Projects/navi-cheats/cheats </dev/tty ) " ) " ; then LBUFFER = " $selected " fi zle redisplay } zle -N _call_navi bindkey '^n' _call_navi

后重要部分是--path参数，它告诉navi在特定位置查找备忘单（我更喜欢使用我自己的目录，而不是内置目录和“添加回购”功能）。 您可以添加多个
通过:分隔的目录（从技术上讲，我有几个目录，包括我从上面的示例中提取的$work目录）。

上面列出的路径是我的公共仓库的cheats/目录。 它只是.cheat文件。 很快就会有更多内容。

这很有用，因为我经常想对我的备忘单没有说明的命令进行一些额外的编辑，或者这些功能根本不存在以增加我想要的灵活性。

通常，-- --print只会将命令打印到stdout ，但是使用 ZSH，您可以将当前命令行替换为命令的输出，这就是函数其余部分所发生的事情。

什么是.cheat文件？

我不会重新散列navi存储库中的整个教程，因为它非常好且全面，但 TL;DR 是：

  • 我倾向于使用命令名称或情况作为文件名。
  • 以;开头的行被忽略（评论）
  • 以%开头的行是类别和逗号分隔的关键词。 这些在模糊匹配时会成为很好的“标签”。 我倾向于使用命令名称或名称的变体来帮助匹配。
  • 命令描述是以#开头的行，用作命令的简短描述
  • 命令模板位于描述下方的行中，没有前导字符。
    • 命令模板可以是多行的\就像常规的 shell 命令一样
    • 变量是用尖括号包围的单词（即<foo> ）
  • 所以我们的<foo>变量可以有一个扩展$ foo: echo -e "true false"这将使用fzf向用户显示两个选项true和false ，然后无论用户选择什么都会替换命令中的<foo>模板。
  • 如果没有提供变量扩展行，则会向用户显示一个fzf文本框，允许他们键入他们想要的任何内容，这将替换模板中的变量。
  • 文件可以有任意多的“类别”
  • 多个命令模板可以共享一个扩展。 即想到一堆防火墙命令，所有这些命令都需要允许用户选择端口号。 所有防火墙模板命令都可以列出一个<port>变量，并且该文件将包含一个$ port: ...适用于所有模板的扩展。
  • 除了用作标签之外，类别还分离出变量扩展。 因此变量扩展仅适用于同一类别中的命令模板。

所以一个典型的.cheat文件遵循这种模式：

 % foo, bar # Description of command command -f -b <qux> # Description of other command other_command -b -z <bar> <foo> $ bar: ... $ foo: ...

同样，单个.cheat文件可以有多个%类别，拆分扩展，以允许您重复使用具有不同具体扩展的变量名。 虽然，我通常每个文件只有一个% 。

变量扩展

这就是navi的真正力量发挥作用的地方。 默认情况下， navi会为没有扩展行的变量显示一个空白的fzf文本框。 比如上面的<qux>变量。

因此，让我们首先构建我们的第一个strace .cheat文件：

 % strace # Display system calls for a command strace <CMD>

运行navi ，并在模糊匹配区域中键入一些内容以选择我们的strace命令，然后会弹出一个新屏幕，要求我们填写CMD变量（因为我们没有提供扩展）。 如果我们输入firefox ， navi我们带回终端，并准备好运行以下命令：

静态扩展

我们可以为用户提供一组有限的选项（默认情况下也允许他们输入我们提供的选项之外的内容）。

因此，如果我们改为在命令模板下方添加以下行，我们将获得包含两个选项的列表。

 $ CMD: echo -e 'firefox
google-chrome'

笔记：
用户仍然可以输入我们两个选项之外的内容，尽管我们可以阻止这种行为，我们将在一分钟内看到。

由于默认情况下fzf通过换行符分隔选项 ，我们实际上可以使用空格使扩展更具可读性，因为值本身没有任何空格。

 $ CMD: echo 'firefox google-chrome' | tr ' ' '
'

这在功能上等同于我们之前拥有的，但有些人更喜欢它。 它还指出扩展可以使用标准管道| 让我们在文件中添加一个新命令（带有描述）：

 % strace # Display system calls for a command strace <CMD> ; 👇 is new # Display system calls for a single PID strace -p <PID> $ CMD: echo 'firefox google-chrome' | tr ' ' '
'

现在我们可以输入一个我们希望附加strace的 PID。

但有个问题; 由于我们没有提供扩展，因此我们需要输入有效的 PID。 例如，输入foo只是盲目地使我们的命令行：

运行时会出错。

使用静态扩展可以在一定程度上帮助解决这个问题：

注意：在 64 位系统上， PID_MAX_LIMIT大约为 400 万...但为了简单和演示，我们将其设为 32 位限制，因为我们很快就会更改它。

 $ PID: echo {0..32768} | tr ' ' '
'

哪个更好，我们会得到一个巨大的数字列表（我们可以模糊匹配）。 我们将使用 PID 进入命令行：

但是我们仍然遇到有人可以键入foo的问题，我们再次处于无效区域。

一个这样的选项是--prevent-extra ，它将用户限制为您提供的选项列表，仅此而已。

笔记：
当用户提供显示列表之外的内容时， navi会出错并退出。 我对这是否更好持观望态度。 但这同样是一个选择。

将我们的扩展更改为以下内容解决了我们之前遇到的无效问题：

 ; 👇 is new $ PID: echo {0..32768} | tr ' ' '
' --- --prevent-extra

动态扩展

那个没有上下文的疯狂 PID 列表也没什么用。 幸运的是navi可以动态地提供选项！

因此，我们不仅可以按顺序提供 32,000 个数字，没有进程名称，甚至不知道它是否是当前进程的有效 PID，我们还可以让navi通过标准 shell 命令查看当前正在运行的进程，
将这些呈现给用户。

但是我们有一个小问题，比如ps -aux打印出的信息比我们需要的多得多。 我们或多或少需要一个 PID 和一个名称。

由于navi扩展只是 shell 管道，我们可以使用grep和awk等标准实用程序来解决这个问题！

 $ PID: ps -aux | grep -v PID | awk '{print $2" "$11}' --- --prevent-extra

我们保留--prevent-extra是因为如果 PID 不在此列表中，我们仍然会受到限制，那么它是无效的。

现在我们得到了一个很好的 PID 和相关进程的模糊匹配列表！

然而，运行它显示了另一个问题......我们在命令行上获得了整个选择：

 $ strace -p 3860 /usr/bin/firefox

笔记：
那里的额外空间也来自我们的扩展选项！

有一个--column选项允许我们只使用一列输出作为最终选择！

将我们的扩展更改为：

 ; 👇 is new $ PID: ps -aux | grep -v PID | awk '{print $2" "$11}' --- --column 1 --prevent-extra

给我们正确的输出。

好多了！

添加剂选项

好的，所以我们现在可以构建一个strace命令，通过我们计算机上运行的有效进程列表进行模糊匹配，但是strace为我们提供了各种附加选项。 例如，我们想要抑制attach/detach消息（即-q ）或其变体（ -qq或-qqq ）？

事实证明，我们也可以为此使用扩展！

稍微更改我们的命令模板，为-q|-qq|-qqq添加一个占位符：

 # Display system calls for a single PID strace <QUIET> -p <PID>

然后我们可以添加另一个扩展这个变量：

 $ QUIET: echo -e '		No suppression;-q		Suppress attach/detach messages;-qq		Also suppress exit statuses;-qqq		Suppress every suppressible message' | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1

我们只是在建立我们已经知道的东西。 将选项分成两列，左边是我们要添加的标志，右边是描述，用;分隔所有选项我们可以通过tr将其替换为换行符，
然后告诉navi只使用第一列作为扩展的实际选择输出。

现在我们得到一个新屏幕，询问我们是否要抑制消息，左侧有相关选项！

专业提示：最好首先放置代表不附加标志的“none”或“no”选项，因为当用户不想添加任何附加标志时，他们可以直接按<enter>飞过。 当有一堆可选标志时，这会很有帮助。

我想添加的最后一个细节是--header选项，它允许提供一个简短的文本来描述用户实际选择的内容。 所以我们最终的扩展版本变成了：

 $ QUIET: echo -e '		No suppression;-q		Suppress attach/detach messages;-qq		Also suppress exit statuses;-qqq		Suppress every suppressible message' | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Suppress any output?"

这会在输入框上方显示一条文本消息，其中描述了navi是什么
问。

我们可以继续充实选项，为我们想要呈现的标志重复上述步骤。

这是更完整的扩展集：

 # Display system calls for a command strace <SUMMARY> <QUIET> <FOLLOW_FORKS> <SYSCALL_TIMES> <SUCCESS_FAILED> <DECODE_FDS> <CMD> ; 👆 these variables are new # Display system calls for a single PID strace <SUMMARY> <QUIET> <FOLLOW_FORKS> <SYSCALL_TIMES> <SUCCESS_FAILED> <DECODE_FDS> -p <PID> ; 👆 these variables are new $ PID: ps aux | grep -v 'PID' | awk '{print $2" "$11}' --- --column 1 --header "PID to attach to?" $ QUIET: echo -e '		No suppression;-q		Suppress attach/detach messages;-qq		Also suppress exit statuses;-qqq		Suppress every suppressible message' | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Suppress any output?" ; 👇 These expansions are new $ TIME_STAMPS: echo -e "-t		absolute;-tt		absolute usecs;-ttt		absolute UNIX time" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Print Timestamps?" $ SUMMARY: echo -e "		none;-c		summary;-C		summary only" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Print Summary?" $ FOLLOW_FORKS: echo -e "		no;-f		follow forks;-ff		follow forks and split output" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Follow Forks?" $ SYSCALL_TIMES: echo -e "		no;-T		time spent in syscalls" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Display time spent in syscalls?" $ SUCCESS_FAILED: echo -e "		don't filter;-z		success only;-Z		failed only" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Filter success or failed exits?" $ DECODE_FDS: echo -e "		no;-yy		decode all fds" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Decode File Descriptors?"

现在我们将有更多的扩展，您会注意到在navi窗口的上半部分，有一个扩展的预览，显示了哪些扩展已扩展到 var。 这有时会派上用场。

限制

这给我们带来了navi的局限性之一（到目前为止），因为很难看出先前的变量是否已扩展为某物或另一物。 有可变的依赖关系，但那些并不完全符合我的要求。

一个很好的例子是strace的下一个（可能也是最重要的）选项，那就是过滤。

例如，要查看与进程相关的所有系统调用（例如创建进程时的execve(2) ），您可以使用：

 $ strace -e trace=process ...

有很多预定义选项，以及值、组等。

所以我们最终得到了两个不同的命令模板，一个有表达式，一个没有。

此外， navi仅限于单个表达式，例如，您不能循环并提供多个表达式。 防火墙端口），但不是strace期望的语法中的多对。 这通常很好，因为您总是可以在navi完成后添加其他表达式，而且很多时候您只需要一个表达式即可。

为了更好地理解这一点，我们将再添加两个命令模板，添加表达式，以及navi提供的options和values对列表：

 # Display system calls for a command (with a filter) strace -e <EXPR_OPT>=<EXPR_SET> <SUMMARY> <QUIET> <FOLLOW_FORKS> <SYSCALL_TIMES> <SUCCESS_FAILED> <DECODE_FDS> <CMD> ; 👆 the -e variables are new # Display system calls for a single PID (with a filter) strace -e <EXPR_OPT>=<EXPR_SET> <SUMMARY> <QUIET> <FOLLOW_FORKS> <SYSCALL_TIMES> <SUCCESS_FAILED> <DECODE_FDS> -p <PID> ; 👆 the -e variables are new $ EXPR_OPT: echo 'trace abbrev verbose raw signal read write fault inject status quiet kvm decode-fds' | tr ' ' '
' --- --header "Expression Option (or type custom)?" $ EXPR_SET: echo 'clock creds desc file fstat fstatfs ipc llstat memory net process pure signal stat statfs all none' | tr ' ' '
' --- --header "Expression Set (or type custom)?"

现在我们可以交互式地构建一个完整的strace命令！

我们希望允许用户输入我们列表之外的内容，因为strace支持更多的选项，以及正则表达式，或者! 不是表达式等。但是， navi没有完整的方式来表达这些选项，所以我们受到限制。

但这总比没有好！

结论

希望这表明拥有命令生成器可以提供巨大的帮助。 是的......制作备忘单是一项工作，但是有很多可供复制的成熟方法。 我更喜欢使用我自己的，并且对它们有更多的控制权，但是对于那些想要引导自己制作作弊的人来说，这个选项是存在的。

快乐作弊。



原文:

Diving into navi as an interactive command builder, and using strace as a demo for concise cheat sheet!

In the previous post I spent a short time detailing steps I take to make my command line experience more pleasant and efficient. One of the steps I listed was using the tool navi to quickly recall
commands.

This is incredibly helpful for commands that I don't use often, or for variations of commands with specific options that I use less frequently. I find fuzzy searching through my navi cheats is 100x faster than grepping through
Manpages or --help output. Now, of course there are times where my navi cheats don't cover something I need and I must fall back to older methods, but then I just update my cheats and go about my day.

I recently found a new (to me) way to use navi, as a "command builder" of sorts. It makes sense to use on commands where the flags/options are additive, and not individual commands (which is somewhat of a pet peeve of mine...)

¯\(°_o)/¯

I should make a post on CLI UX about why certain CLI patterns are sub-optimal...

Enter strace

A good example is strace, which allows one to display system calls made by particular commands and processes. Several other blog posts have recently mentioned strace, and it's a tool that has come in hand for me more than a
couple times so in it's honor it'll be the demo today.

We won't be adding all the options that strace has, but the ones I find most common to my use cases, which probably covers a pretty vast swath of people as well.

Our goal is to build a navi cheat sheet that walks us through building an strace command with all the options we want to expose.

This post won't cover all the details of strace or even what all the options mean, although most of the ones we're adding are pretty self explanatory.

(ᕗ ͠° ਊ ͠° )ᕗ

Like a good CLI should be!

For a pretty good summary, see "strace The Sysadmins
Microscope".

navi Overview

As mentioned in my previous post, I bind navi to <CTRL>-n in my terminal, using the following ZSH function in my ~/.zshrc:

_call_navi() { local selected if selected="$(printf "$(navi --print --path ${HOME}/Projects/navi-cheats/cheats </dev/tty)")"; then LBUFFER="$selected" fi zle redisplay
} zle -N _call_navi
bindkey '^n' _call_navi

The post important parts are the --path argument, which tells navi to look in a specific location for cheat sheets (I prefer to use my own directories, instead of the built in ones and "adding repos" feature). You can add multiple
directories separated via : (and technically I have a few directories, including a $work one that I pulled out of the example above).

The path listed above is the cheats/ directory of my public repository. It's just a big list of .cheat files. More on that soon.

Also the --print flag tells navi to simply print the final command, instead of running it. This is useful because often I want to make some additional edits to the command that either my cheat sheet didn't account for, or the features simply don't exist to add the flexibility I wanted.

Normally, --print would just print the command to stdout, however with ZSH you can have the current command line replaced by the output of a command, which is what is happening the remainder of the function.

What is a .cheat file?

navi looks for files ending in .cheat with a particular structure. I won't re-hash the entire tutorial from the navi repository since it's quite good and comprehensive, but the TL;DR is:

  • .cheat files can be split up and named however you like, as the file names aren't used by navi. I tend to use either command names or situations as file names.
  • Lines starting with ; are ignored (comments)
  • Lines starting with % are categories and comma separated key words. These make good "tags" when fuzzy matching. I tend to use command names, or variations of the names to assist with matching.
  • Command descriptions are lines starting with # and used as a short description of a command
  • Commands templates are on the line below a description with no leading characters.
    • Commands templates can be multi-line with \ like regular shell commands
    • KEY Commands templates can have "variables" that get expanded later if the command is selected. Variables are words surrounded by angle brackets (i.e. <foo>)
  • "variable expansions" are optional, but are lines that start with $ followed by the variable name, then : followed by the command to expand the variable (i.e. the command to present options to user for selection). So our <foo> variable could have an expansion $ foo: echo -e "true false" which would present the two options true and false to the user using fzf, then whatever the user selected would replace the <foo> in the command template.
  • If no variable expansion line is provided, an fzf text box is presented to the user allowing them to type whatever they want, which will replace the variable in the template.
  • Files can have as many "categories" as you like
  • Multiple command templates can share a single expansion. I.e. think of of a bunch of firewall commands, all of which need to allow a user to select a port number. All firewall template command could list a <port> variable, and the file would contain a single $ port: ... expansion that applies to all templates.
  • Beyond functioning as tags, categories separate out variable expansions. So variables expansions only apply to command templates within the same category.

So a typical .cheat file follows this kind of a pattern:


% foo, bar

# Description of command
command -f -b <qux>

# Description of other command
other_command -b -z <bar> <foo>

$ bar: ...
$ foo: ...

Again, a single .cheat file can have multiple % categories, splitting up the expansions, to allow you to re-use variable names with different concrete expansions. Although, I normally only have a single % per file.

Variable Expansions

This is where the true power of navi comes in to play. By default, navi presents a blank fzf text box for variables without an expansion line. Such as the <qux> variable above.

So let's first build out our first strace .cheat file:

% strace

# Display system calls for a command
strace <CMD>

Running navi, and typing something in the fuzzy match area to select our strace command then brings up a new screen asking us to fill in the CMD variable (since we did not provide an expansion). If we type firefox, navi drops us back to the terminal with the following command ready to run:

Static Expansions

We can present a limited set of options for the user (which by default also allows them to type something outside of our presented options).

So if we instead add the following line below our command template, we'll get a list of two options.

$ CMD: echo -e 'firefox
google-chrome'

NOTE:
the user can still type something outside of our two options, although we can prevent this behavior which we'll see in a minute.

Since fzf by default delimits options via a newline , we can actually make the expansion a little more readable using spaces, since the values themselves don't have any spaces.

$ CMD: echo 'firefox google-chrome' | tr ' ' '
'

This is functionally equivalent to what we had before, but some prefer it. It also points out that expansions can use standard pipes |.

Let's add a new command (with description) to our file:

% strace

# Display system calls for a command
strace <CMD>

; 👇 is new
# Display system calls for a single PID
strace -p <PID>

$ CMD: echo 'firefox google-chrome' | tr ' ' '
'

Now we can type out a PID we wish to attach strace to.

But there is a problem; since we didn't provide an expansion, it's up to us to type in a valid PID. For example, typing foo just blindly makes our command line:

Which will error when run.

Using a static expansion can help with this somewhat:

NOTE: on 64bit systems PID_MAX_LIMIT is around 4 million...but for simplicity and demo sake we'll make it the 32bit limit since we'll be changing it shortly anyways.

$ PID: echo {0..32768} | tr ' ' '
'

Which is better, we'll get a huge list of numbers (that we can fuzzy match through). And we'll be dropped on the command line with a PID:

But we still have the problem of someone can just type foo and again we're in invalid territory.

navi provides a delimiter we can use on the variable expansions --- to provide additional context navi either about the selection itself, or presentation of the options. One such option is --prevent-extra which limits the user to list of options you provided, and nothing more.

NOTE:
--prevent-extra is listed as experimental. And when user provides something outside the presented list, navi exits with an error. I'm on the fence about if that's better or not. But it's an option no less.

Changing our expansion to the following solves the invalid issue we had before:

;                                    👇 is new
$ PID: echo {0..32768} | tr ' ' '
' --- --prevent-extra

Dynamic Expansions

That crazy list of PIDs with no context isn't a ton of help either. Luckily navi can come up with options dynamically!

So instead of just providing 32,000 numbers in sequence, with no process names, or even knowing if its a valid PID of a current process we can just ask navi to look at the currently running processes via standard shell commands and
present those to the user.

But we have a slight problem, something like ps -aux prints out far more information than we need. We need a PID and a name, more or less.

Since navi expansions are just shell pipes, we can use standard utilities like grep and awk to fix that!

$ PID: ps -aux | grep -v PID | awk '{print $2"    "$11}' --- --prevent-extra

We leave the --prevent-extra because we're still limited on if the PID isn't present in this list, well its not valid.

Now we get a nice fuzzy match-able list of PIDs and the associated processes!

However, running this shows one more issue...we get the whole selection on our command line:

$ strace -p 3860    /usr/bin/firefox

NOTE:
the extra spaces in there come from our expansion option too!

navi options to the rescue again! There is a --column option which allows us to use only a single column of output as the final selection!

Changing our expansion to:

;                                                            👇 is new
$ PID: ps -aux | grep -v PID | awk '{print $2"    "$11}' --- --column 1 --prevent-extra

Gives us the correct output.

Much better!

Additive Options

Ok, so we can now build an strace command, fuzzy match through a valid list of processes running on our computer, but strace provides all kinds of additional options for us. For example, what we want to suppress attach/detach messages (i.e. -q), or it's variations (-qq or -qqq)?

Turns out, we can use expansions for that too!

Changing our command template slightly, to add a placeholder for the -q|-qq|-qqq:

# Display system calls for a single PID
strace <QUIET> -p <PID>

We can then add another expansion this variable:

$ QUIET: echo -e '		No suppression;-q		Suppress attach/detach messages;-qq		Also suppress exit statuses;-qqq		Suppress every suppressible message' | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1

We're just building off what we already know. Splitting options into two columns with the flag we want to add on the left, and description on the right, separating all options with ; which we can replace with a newline via tr,
then telling navi to only use the first column as the actual selected output for the expansion.

Now we get a new screen asking us if we want to suppress messages or not, with the associated option on the left!

PRO TIP: It's a good idea to place the "none" or "no" option that represents no additional flags first, as the user can then just fly through pressing <enter> when they don't wish to add anything additional. This greatly helps when there are a bunch of optional flags.

One final nicety I like to add is the --header option, which allows one to give a short text describing what the user is actually selecting. This comes in handy sometimes when the choices aren't super explanatory.

So our final expansion version becomes:

$ QUIET: echo -e '		No suppression;-q		Suppress attach/detach messages;-qq		Also suppress exit statuses;-qqq		Suppress every suppressible message' | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Suppress any output?"

This shows a text message above the input box with description of what navi is
asking.

We can continue fleshing out the options repeating the above steps for the flags we want to present.

Here is more complete set of expansions:

# Display system calls for a command
strace <SUMMARY> <QUIET> <FOLLOW_FORKS> <SYSCALL_TIMES> <SUCCESS_FAILED> <DECODE_FDS> <CMD>
;      👆 these variables are new
# Display system calls for a single PID
strace <SUMMARY> <QUIET> <FOLLOW_FORKS> <SYSCALL_TIMES> <SUCCESS_FAILED> <DECODE_FDS> -p <PID>
;      👆 these variables are new

$ PID: ps aux | grep -v 'PID' | awk '{print $2"    "$11}' --- --column 1 --header "PID to attach to?"
$ QUIET: echo -e '		No suppression;-q		Suppress attach/detach messages;-qq		Also suppress exit statuses;-qqq		Suppress every suppressible message' | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Suppress any output?"

; 👇 These expansions are new
$ TIME_STAMPS: echo -e "-t		absolute;-tt		absolute usecs;-ttt		absolute UNIX time" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Print Timestamps?"
$ SUMMARY: echo -e "		none;-c		summary;-C		summary only" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Print Summary?"
$ FOLLOW_FORKS: echo -e "		no;-f		follow forks;-ff		follow forks and split output" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Follow Forks?"
$ SYSCALL_TIMES: echo -e "		no;-T		time spent in syscalls" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Display time spent in syscalls?"
$ SUCCESS_FAILED: echo -e "		don't filter;-z		success only;-Z		failed only" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Filter success or failed exits?"
$ DECODE_FDS: echo -e "		no;-yy		decode all fds" | tr ';' '
' --- --column 1 --header "Decode File Descriptors?"

Now that we'll have more expansions, you'll notice that in the upper section of the navi window, there is a preview of what expansions have been expanded to thus var. This comes in handy at times.

Limitations

This brings us to one of the limitations of navi (thus far), in that it's difficult to see if a previous variable has been expanded into something or another. There is variable dependencies but those don't quite do what I want.

A good example is the next (and probably most important) option for strace and that is the filtering.

For example, so see all syscalls related to processes (such as execve(2) for when a process is created), you could use:

$ strace -e trace=process ...

The -e option takes expressions in the form of option=value. There are quite a few predefined options, as well as values, groups, etc.

The way navi works now, you can't for example ask, "Do you want to add an expression" and insert -e foo=bar or nothing if they answer no. So we end up with two different command templates, one with expressions and one without.

Additionally, navi is limited to a single expression, you can't for example loop and provide multiple expressions. navi does provide a --multi option which allows FZF to pick multiple options from the list (i.e. imagine multiple
firewall ports), but not multiple pairs in the syntax strace expects. This is normally fine, as you can always add additional expressions after navi is finished, and many times a single expression is all you want anyways.

To get a better sense of this, we'll add two more command templates, adding expressions, and navi provided lists for the options and values pairs:

# Display system calls for a command (with a filter)
strace -e <EXPR_OPT>=<EXPR_SET> <SUMMARY> <QUIET> <FOLLOW_FORKS> <SYSCALL_TIMES> <SUCCESS_FAILED> <DECODE_FDS> <CMD>
;      👆 the -e variables are new
# Display system calls for a single PID (with a filter)
strace -e <EXPR_OPT>=<EXPR_SET> <SUMMARY> <QUIET> <FOLLOW_FORKS> <SYSCALL_TIMES> <SUCCESS_FAILED> <DECODE_FDS> -p <PID>
;      👆 the -e variables are new

$ EXPR_OPT: echo 'trace abbrev verbose raw signal read write fault inject status quiet kvm decode-fds' | tr ' ' '
' --- --header "Expression Option (or type custom)?"
$ EXPR_SET: echo 'clock creds desc file fstat fstatfs ipc llstat memory net process pure signal stat statfs all none' | tr ' ' '
' --- --header "Expression Set (or type custom)?"

Now we can interactively build a full strace command!

We want to allow the user to type something outside our list, because strace supports a bunch more options, and regex, or ! not expressions, etc. However, navi doesn't have a full way to express those options so we're limited.

But this is still leaps and bounds better than nothing!

Conclusion

Hopefully, this has shown how having a command builder can be a huge help. Yes...it is work to make the cheat sheets, however there are plenty out there ripe for copying. navi also has built in functionality to add public git
repos as cheat repositories, and have them automatically updated. I prefer to use my own and have more control over them, but that option exists for those that want to bootstrap making their own cheats.

There is quite a bit more navi can do, but this post is long enough!

Happy cheating.
item.author: kbknapp

item.title: AWS Community Builder: ¿Qué es? AWS 社区建设者：有什么问题？ y ¿Cómo puedes formar parte? y ¿Cómo puedes formar parte？ | AWS Community Builder: ¿Qué es? y ¿Cómo puedes formar parte?
item.description:
雪拉·利科克

发表于数据 (Base de datos, análisis y BI)

  • 竞争者

  • 开发者的教育机构

  • 核行动

  • 游戏技术

  • 机器学习

  • 无服务器

  • 阿尔马塞纳米托

  • 工具包 de recursos gráficos que puedes utilizar en tus redes sociales y cuando compartas contenido。

  • 邀请您来参加：拆箱 AWS Community Builders Swag Welcome Kit

    • 订阅 Cloud Academy 的在线学习年度订阅，帮助您了解课程、实验室和实践项目。

    • 领英： https ://www.linkedin.com/in/sheyla-leacock/
      推特： https ://twitter.com/Sheynnie_Mcr
      加入 DEV 和 MongoDB，并使用 MongoDB Atlas 和 Atlas Search 为DEV x MongoDB Atlas Hackathon 2022构建具有全文搜索功能的应用程序。

      拉希鲁·赫瓦瓦萨姆—— 一旦暂停，sheyla_leacock 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被删除。

    • 解除暂停后，sheyla_leacock 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

    一旦取消发布，sheyla_leacock 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

    如果 sheyla_leacock 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

    感谢您保护 DEV 社区 👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 以下是标记 sheyla_leacock 的方法：

    报告其他不当行为

    取消标记 sheyla_leacock 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



    原文:

    Sheyla Leacock

    Posted on Nov 27

    Seguramente en alguna ocasión has escuchado mencionar el programa AWS Community Builders, pero sabes ¿qué es y en que consiste?. Te comparto un poco de este programa desde mi experiencia.

    "El programa AWS Community Builders ofrece recursos técnicos, educación y oportunidades de creación de redes para los entusiastas técnicos de AWS y los líderes intelectuales emergentes apasionados por compartir conocimientos y conectarse con la comunidad técnica."

    ¿Cómo es el proceso de convocatoria y selección?

    El periodo de convocatorias para aquellos interesados en formar parte del programa se realiza dos veces al año. Debes aplicar mediante un formulario en el cual te solicitan información sobre tu interés en formar parte del programa, tu experiencia con la nube de AWS y tus aportes hacia la comunidad. Al ser un programa que potencia la creación de comunidad, los aportes que hayas realizado ya sea escribiendo artículos técnicos, dando charlas, entre otros, pueden ser un punto fundamental a considerar durante el proceso de selección. ¡Ojo! Los aportes que hayas realizado no tienen que ser relacionados a AWS o a la nube, pueden ser temas de tecnología u otros, la idea es que puedas compartir algunas referencias a los contenidos que has creado como aporte a las comunidades. En tu aplicación deberás seleccionar algunas de las categorías en las que te interesaría formar parte. Estas se tomarán en cuenta para asociarte a una de ellas como categoría principal. Los expertos de AWS de cada categoría que elijas revisarán tu aplicación y si no eres seleccionado en la categoría que elegiste como principal, aún tendrás oportunidad de ser considerado en alguna de las categorías que seleccionaste. Estas categorías son más como una forma de asociar tus intereses de aprendizaje y experiencias, sin embargo, una vez que estés dentro del programa, tendrás acceso a las opciones de aprendizaje de todas las categorías y podrás compartir contenido en otras categorías libremente.

    Algunas de las categorías que se mantienen actualmente son las siguientes:

    • datos (Base de datos, análisis y BI)

    • seguridad e identidad (a la cual pertenezco)

    • contenedores

    • herramientas para desarrolladores

    • operación en nube

    • game tech

    • machine learning

    • serverless

    • almacenamiento

    • y otras más.

    Luego del periodo de aplicación, sigue el proceso de revisión de propuestas y selección de los Community Builders a ingresar en cada edición donde un equipo de expertos de AWS hace una revisión detallada de cada aplicación y comunica a los seleccionados. Las ediciones son anuales por lo que serás seleccionado para ser parte del programa durante un año, aunque tendrás la opción de renovar tu aplicación (más adelante te doy más detalle de este punto).

    ¿Qué sucede luego de ser aceptado en el programa?

    Una vez eres aceptado,deberás aceptar un acuerdo de confidencialidad (NDA) ya que tendrás acceso a cierta información exclusiva para los Community Builders.
    Luego de esto recibirás toda la información necesaria del programa como los líderes de la categoría asignada, todos los beneficios y la información para participar de la sesión de Bienvenida al programa. ¡A celebrar!

    ¿Que beneficios obtengo como Community Builder?

    Este programa ofrece un gran número de beneficios que te comparto a continuación:

    • Sesiones de aprendizaje frecuentes de la mano de expertos de AWS.

    • Créditos promocionales de AWS para que puedas poner en práctica todo lo que vayas aprendiendo y lo que quieras compartir con la comunidad.

    • Oportunidades de networking con otros entusiastas y expertos en la nube de AWS.

    • Kit de recursos gráficos que puedes utilizar en tus redes sociales y cuando compartas contenido.

    • Espacio colaborativo en slack para conocer, interactuar y generar diversas oportunidades con otros profesionales.

    • El Swag Welcome Kit...Un Kit de bienvenida al programa que trae unos artículos super cool. Te muestro los que me llegaron en esta edición 2022:

    PD: También grabé un video unboxing de este welcome kit. Te invito a verlo en este enlace: Unboxing AWS Community Builders Swag Welcome Kit

    • Conocer de primera mano información de nuevos servicios o funcionalidades antes de que sean lanzadas oficialmente los clientes, así como la oportunidad de poder probarlas y brindar tu feedback ;).

    • Voucher para cubrir el 100% de un examen de certificación de AWS de tu elección.

    • Suscripción anual de e-learning en Cloud Academy para aprender sobre la nube con cursos, laboratorios y entornos de práctica.

    • La oportunidad de obtener otros premios buenísimos por tus aportes a la comunidad y avances de aprendizaje :D

    • La oportunidad de renovar tu aplicación como Community Builder para ser elegido por un año más y así sucesivamente para seguir formando parte del programa. Así mismo, se renueva tu Kit de bienvenida (con artículos diferentes a los que has recibido en años anteriores), tu suscripción de Cloud Academy y tus Vouchers de certificación así como todos los otros beneficios ;D.

    Recuerda que si te interesa formar parte del programa puedes agregarte a la lista de espera para que recibas una notificación cuando abra la nueva edición del programa.

    Puedes conectar conmigo en:
    Linkedin: https://www.linkedin.com/in/sheyla-leacock/
    Twitter: https://twitter.com/Sheynnie_Mcr
    Youtube: https://www.youtube.com/@sheynnie

    Join DEV and MongoDB and build an application with full-text search capabilities using MongoDB Atlas and Atlas Search for the DEV x MongoDB Atlas Hackathon 2022.

    → Join the Hackathon

    Lahiru Hewawasam - Oct 31

    Once suspended, sheyla_leacock will not be able to comment or publish posts until their suspension is removed.

    Once unsuspended, sheyla_leacock will be able to comment and publish posts again.

    Once unpublished, all posts by sheyla_leacock will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

    If sheyla_leacock is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

    Thanks for keeping DEV Community 👩‍💻👨‍💻 safe. Here is what you can do to flag sheyla_leacock:

item.title: 我的外壳设置 | My Shell Setup
item.description:
凯文·K。

发表于详细介绍了我为使我的终端体验更愉快而采取的一些步骤。

我一天中的大部分时间都以一种或另一种形式在终端机上度过。 多年来我收集了一些步骤，使我的体验更愉快，最重要的是更有效率。

大多数步骤需要安装某种级别的软件或更改配置，因此我无法在我工作的所有生产服务器上执行这些步骤。但是，许多步骤仅适用于我的本地机器，并且我在远程服务器上工作的时间很短，或者在本地所做的更改的帮助下。

配置

在我谈论实际步骤之前，我应该提到我存储自定义配置文件的位置。 在我希望配置的每台机器上手动输入这些将是巨大的痛苦。

像许多人一样，我将所有配置文件都存储在dotfiles上的公共点文件存储库中。 这有点乱，因为我只在特定机器上使用特定部件。 所以不是
旨在公开阅读或分析。 然而，我的观点是配置文件存储在一个中央位置，我可以简单地将存储库克隆到我的机器上，符号链接我关心和想要使用的文件或目录并开始我的一天。

我不使用 Ansible 之类的东西来设置我的机器，因为有太多变量可以控制我如何使用特定机器以及我想实际使用哪些步骤。

旁白：我其实有一个baseline
一组脚本，它们根据我填写的 TUI 对话框为我完成所有复制/符号链接。 需要明确的是，这绝不意味着要公开使用，因为许多步骤都是针对我的特定机器进行硬编码的。

发行须知

我在我所有的工作站上都​​使用 Fedora，但我有时也会运行 Ubuntu 的变体来工作，所以所有的步骤都适用于任一发行版。 这篇文章中列出的安装命令适用于 Fedora。 并且我会尝试在我知道有任何差异的地方指出来。

改进

这些步骤在某种程度上改变了我与终端交互的方式，无论它们是独立的工具，还是我使用的工作流程。 所有这些步骤都用于提高我的工作速度，或者改善体验。

大多数更改都是基于彼此或我多年来积累的其他技能，因此它们可能不会广泛适用，除非你在类似的船上。 例如，在我的 shell 中使用插件vi-mode结合fzf搜索
通过我的命令历史是闪电般的快速（特别是当我需要编辑以前的命令时）。

虽然默认的 Bash shell 在许多情况下（并且无处不在）都很好，但它肯定有其局限性。 许多人都熟悉并喜欢更现代的鱼壳。 然而，我发现 Fish 破坏了我现有脚本的太多（好吧，几乎所有脚本）以及我的肌肉记忆，并且导致我在谷歌搜索修复时不得不更改命令，因为每个人都假设 Bash（或至少是 Sh ).

除此之外，大多数人对 Fish 赞不绝口，至少在最初，是自动建议和语法高亮显示，我喜欢的 shell (ZSH) 可以做的和您很快就会看到的一样好。

我所有的 Bash 知识都适用于 ZSH。

旁白：我个人遇到差异的一个领域是在为程序编写完成脚本时（即当您点击<tab><tab>时弹出的菜单）。 定期或根本不完成脚本，所以这不应该影响他们。

因为我在旁边提到了完成脚本，所以支持 ZSH 完成的程序比 Bash 完成变体好得多。

这是在 Bash 中完成git的示例：

下面是在 ZSH 中完成git的一个例子：

有很多我喜欢的 ZSH 功能，但有几个听起来最小但我可能使用最多的是不区分大小写的路径、命令修复建议和不使用cd更改目录。

那些听起来很小。 但是当不断使用终端时，它们会迅速加起来。

在 ZSH 中，你可以输入cat foo ，当文件真的是Foo时，ZSH 会自动为你更正路径。

如果你打错了命令，并且它足够接近真实的命令，或者文件/目录，ZSH 也会建议一个你可以接受或忽略的修复。 例如：

 $ gti commit -m "foo" zsh: correct 'gti' to 'git' [nyae]?

最后，不用输入cd ../blah/你可以直接输入../blah/ 。

尽管 ZSH 可以做的事情还有很多，但我们假设您已售出，我们如何安装和使用它？ 这是我的设置：

 $ sudo dnf install zsh (install ZSH) $ sudo chsh -s $(which zsh) $USER (change my default shell to ZSH)

注意： chsh在某些发行版中默认不再可用。 在 Fedora 中，它包含在包util-linux-user中。

然后我使用以下~/.zshrc配置：

 ENABLE_CORRECTION = "true" COMPLETION_WAITING_DOTS = "true" export PATH = /usr/local/bin: $HOME /.local/bin: $PATH export SSH_KEY_PATH = "~/.ssh/rsa_id" export TERM = "xterm-256color" fpath+ = ~/.zfunc compinit -U

哦-我的-ZSH

有一个 ZSH 框架
添加了大量有用的功能，称为oh-my-zsh 。 它还支持主题化你的提示之类的东西，但我并没有真正使用那部分，因为我依赖

在其他提示软件上。

要安装，请克隆存储库并向~/.zshrc添加几行：

 $ git clone https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh ~/.oh-my-zsh

然后在 ZSH 配置文件的顶部添加几行：

 export ZSH = $HOME /.oh-my-zsh ZSH_THEME = "robbyrussell" plugins =( git systemd ) source $ZSH /oh-my-zsh.sh

但是，在以下步骤中，我禁用它以支持其他选项。

有大量的插件，上面列出的只是一些适用于几乎所有盒子的插件。 在接下来的部分中，我们将添加更多内容。 许多插件只是为特定命令添加 shell 完成，而其他插件则添加全新的功能。 它是
绝对值得浏览一下插件列表，看看是否有任何吸引您的眼球是您经常使用的软件。

笔记
与插件相关的启动成本很小，因此最好只添加您经常使用的插件。

自动建议

这是大多数人喜欢 Fish 的功能。 它使用您的命令历史记录在您键入时实时建议命令，坦率地说，我也喜欢它。

要将此功能添加到 ZSH，您需要克隆一个存储库并添加一个插件。

 $ git clone https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-autosuggestions ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-~/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/plugins/zsh-autosuggestions

然后将zsh-autosuggestions添加到~/.zshrc中的plugins数组

语法高亮

人们喜欢 Fish 的另一个功能。 如果找到并有效该命令，则将您输入的命令着色为绿色，否则为红色。 这再次听起来很小，但是在键入您不经常使用的命令或
从其他媒体复制/粘贴时。 它甚至可以在运行命令之前捕获拼写错误。 我喜欢它。

同样，一个简单的克隆并添加一个插件。

 $ git clone https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-syntax-highlighting.git ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-~/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/plugins/zsh-syntax-highlighting

然后将zsh-syntax-highlighting添加到plugins数组。

缩写别名

这个插件自动扩展别名。 例如，我有一个别名gl='git log --graph --all --oneline --decorate' 。 使用此插件，当我键入gl后跟<space> （假设我想添加到命令中）或
你为什么要这个？！ 因为人们常常记住别名，而不是它所代表的命令。 这意味着当您通过 SSH 连接到服务器或在没有所有自定义别名的机器上时，您会感到处于劣势。 使用
要安装，您克隆一个 repo 并在您的~/.zshrc source获取脚本，并使用abbrev-alias而不是普通的alias命令。

 $ git clone https://github.com/momo-lab/zsh-abbrev-alias ~/.config/zsh-abbrev-alias/ $ echo 'source $HOME/.config/zsh-abbrev-alias/abbrev-alias.plugin.zsh' >> ~/.zshrc

然后例如，添加我之前提到的gl别名

abbrev-alias gl = 'git log --graph --all --oneline --decorate'

自由使用别名是快速使用终端的一个关键方面。 这个插件消除了为什么有些人不喜欢别名的最大危害！

笔记：
我仍然使用普通别名的唯一一次是，如果我用另一个命令替换一个命令，即alias ls=exa并且两个命令上的标志/选项兼容（或几乎兼容）。

须藤

生活质量的另一个微小改进是能够快速将sudo添加到任何命令的开头。 如果我在输入一个长命令的中途意识到我忘记添加sudo ，在 Bash 中我可能必须删除命令并
重新开始，或将光标移动到行首以键入sudo并返回末尾，或者如果该命令可以安全地以非特权用户身份运行，则只需运行它然后快速运行sudo !! 重播命令。 然而，所有
这些都不是很好，而且可能很慢。

这个插件允许我简单地键入<esc><esc>来添加sudo并让我的光标保持在我有它的地方。 作为奖励，它甚至不会对也使用<esc> vi-mode （我们接下来会看到！）产生负面影响。

要安装，只需将sudo添加到plugins数组中，因为它内置于oh-my-zsh中。

虚拟模式

此插件仅在您习惯 Vi(m) 键绑定时才有用。 我经常使用它。 当与下面的starship结合使用时，效果会更好，因为如果您处于正常模式或插入模式，您会在提示中看到一个视觉指示器。

要安装，只需将vi-mode添加到plugins数组中，因为它内置于 oh-my-zsh。

自定义提示

自定义提示允许您直观地确定有关您所处环境的事实，而无需运行其他命令。 大多数时候，您可以通过手动命令找到所有这些信息，但为了有效地使用终端，我们尽量减少必须使用的手动命令。

有些人更喜欢将此信息放在其他位置，例如tmux状态行。 但是，我更喜欢在我的 shell 提示符中获得关于我的临时环境的大部分信息。

非临时信息是机器资源信息、代码、时钟等项目。非临时信息我更喜欢在tmux或我的桌面环境 (DE) 等地方，而不是我的提示符。

为了解决长路径或大量信息开始占据当前行的大部分的问题，我更喜欢提示解决方案，它允许多行提示，其中所有“状态信息”都在第一行，我的光标/命令在第二行。

提示有两种很好的解决方案； 我发现 Powerlevel10k 更快，但 Starship 更容易定制，具有一些很棒的功能，也更容易安装。 我使用 Starship
默认值，但有时会切换到 Powerlevel10k。

星舰

演示站点有一个很棒的 GIF，展示了所有功能和原因。 以及如何配置所有不同的项目。 我有一个自定义提示，但默认设置也完全可以接受。

安装：

 $ curl -fsSL https://starship.rs/install.sh | bash

要使用，只需注释掉您的ZSH_THEME变量，并将此行添加到您的~/.zshrc （安装脚本可能已经添加）：

 eval " $( starship init zsh ) "

功率等级10k

要安装我们克隆一个 repo，设置我们的ZSH_THEME并配置一个~/.p10k文件。 当涉及到我想要显示的实际项目时，我发现它的可配置性较低，但我也发现它有时比 starship 更快，因为它是用 ZSH 设计的
请记住（而 starship 旨在与任何外壳一起使用）。

克隆项目：

 $ git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k.git ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-$HOME/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/themes/powerlevel10k

然后我们设置ZSH_THEME="powerlevel10k/powerlevel10k" 。

当我们再次打开终端时，系统会提示我们以交互方式配置一个.p10k文件。

工具

现在我们已经设置了 shell 和提示符，我在终端中经常使用一些工具。 有些与实际的 shell 相关，而另一些只是我认为非常值得安装的通用工具。

终结者、Tilix 或tmux

首先，我会提到一点作弊。 实际的终端仿真器本身。 终端仿真器本身可以在一个方面大大提高一个人的效率。

平铺。

命令到所有终端。

与需要不断在窗口等之间来回移动鼠标的不相交终端相比，这可以极大地提高生产力。

另一种方法是，如果您使用真正的平铺窗口管理器（例如 i3 或tmux ）来处理单个终端会话中的平铺。

笔记：
当然，您可以在 Tilix、i3 中使用tmux ……我觉得那里有一个 Xzibit 笑话。

许多人发现使用 Terminator 或 Tilix 是对平铺的一个很好的介绍，它提供了可用性和功能之间的良好组合，而无需完全致力于tmux或平铺窗口管理器之类的东西。

我个人不会经常使用 Tilix 或 Terminator，因为 i3 会为我处理所有的平铺。 但是，当 SSH 连接到各种服务器并希望平铺窗格而无需重新验证时，我确实使用了tmux 。

模糊的发现者。 这个工具太棒了，它本身值得一篇完整的文章。

它通过列表进行模糊匹配，并允许人们以交互方式选择一个（或多个）项目。 听起来很简单。

当谈到 ZSH 等时，我经常使用的两个功能是ctrl-r模糊搜索我的历史记录和ctrl-t搜索文件路径。

大多数包管理器都包含fzf包，并放置适当的配置文件。 您所要做的就是安装它，并在您的~/.zshrc .zshrc 中获取 ZSH 脚本

$ sudo dnf install fzf $ echo '[ -f ~/.fzf.zsh ] && source ~/.fzf.zsh' >> ~/.zshrc

按<ctrl>-r将显示一个命令列表，您可以通过这些命令进行模糊匹配，按<ctrl>-up/down （或<ctrl>-j/k用于 vim 键绑定）并在选择上按<enter>将该命令粘贴到您的命令行中。

这up按 200 次以在您的历史记录中找到您想要的命令要好得多。

这与<ctrl>-r相同，除了它是一个路径列表。 导航

是的，请。

使用此工具，我可以搜索（并创建我自己的）命令备忘单。 这很难解释，但是当我按下<ctrl>+n时，会弹出一个 TUI（顺便也使用fzf ），我可以通过它进行模糊匹配。 选择命令后，
该命令可以有模板，我可以在其中填写所需的信息。 完成后，我会回到终端并填写完整的命令。 惊人！

这是一张 GIF，比文字更能解释它。 我正在通过firewalld添加一个新的防火墙端口：

我倾向于制作更通用的命令，然后在最后手动调整调用（使用vi-mode非常快）。

安装是通过从源代码构建或使用 bash 完成的：

 $ bash <(curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/denisidoro/navi/master/scripts/install)

制作自己的备忘单超出了本文的范围，但非常值得。 也可以使用其他人的存储库并自动更新它们。 您可以在 GitHub 存储库中找到我自己的表格

默认情况下， navi可以从命令行正常运行。 设置<ctrl>-n键绑定可以通过eval在你的~/.zshrc中使用内置的 widget 命令来完成：

 eval " $( navi widget zsh ) "

默认情况下，键绑定是<ctrl>-g 。 我更喜欢只运行navi widget zsh并将输出粘贴到我的~/.zshrc并将键绑定从g更改为n ，并将它指向我自己的本地作弊存储库。

结论

目前为止就这样了。 我可以写更多（也许以后会）关于我在命令行中经常使用的一些工具，例如exa 、Ripgrep、fd-find 或bat 。 还有可能写下我所做的审美改变
提高我对终端的享受，例如字体选择或我实际使用的终端模拟器。

莱昂纳多蒙蒂尼 - 一旦暂停，kbknapp 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

解除暂停后，kbknapp 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，kbknapp 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 kbknapp 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区 👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 您可以通过以下方式标记 kbknapp：

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 kbknapp 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Kevin K.

Posted on Nov 27

Details a few steps I take to make my terminal experience a little morepleasant.

I spend most of my day in the terminal in one form or another. There are a few steps I've collected over the years to make my experience more pleasant and most importantly more efficient.

Most of the steps require some level of software to be installed or configuration to be changed, so I can't perform these steps on all production servers I work in. However, many of the steps are only applicable to my local machine, and the short period of time I'm working in a remote server is minimal, or aided by changes I made locally.

Configuration

Before I talk about the actual steps, I should mention where I store my custom configuration files. Manually entering these on each machine I wish to configure would be huge pain.

Like many people, I keep all of my configuration files stored in a public dotfiles repository on GitHub. It's a bit of a mess as I only use particular parts on particular machines. So it's not
meant to be publicly read or analyzed. However, my point is that the configuration files are stored in a central location where I can simply clone the repository down to my machine, symlink the files or directories I care about and want to use and go about my day.

I don't use anything like Ansible to set up my machine because there are too many variables that control how I use a particular machine and which steps I want to actually utilize.

Aside: I actually have a baseline
set of scripts that do all the copying/symlinking for me based on a TUI dialog I fill out. To be clear, this is in no way meant to be publicly consumed as many of the steps are hard-coded for my specific machines.

Notes on Distribution

I use Fedora on all my workstations, but I've also run variants of Ubuntu at times for work, so all the steps work on either distro. The install commands listed in this post are for Fedora. The package names may be different in Ubuntu
and I try to call those out where I'm aware of any differences.

Improvements

These steps change some way in which I interact with the terminal, whether they be standalone tools, or work-flows I use. All these steps are used to improve the speed at which I work, or to better the experience.

Most of the changes build on each-other, or other skills I've built up along the years so they may not be widely applicable unless you're in a similar boat. For example, using the plugin vi-mode in my shell combined with fzf searching
through my command history is lightning fast (especially when I need to edit a previous command).

ZSH

While the default Bash shell is fine for many cases (and ubiquitous) it definitely has it's limitations. Many people are familiar with and prefer the more modern Fish shell. However, I find Fish breaks too many of my existing scripts (well, almost all of them) as well as my muscle memory, and causes me to have to change commands when Googling around for fixes because everyone assumes Bash (or Sh at the least).

Add to that, the thing most people ooooh and ahhhhh over with Fish, at least initially, is the auto-suggestions and syntax highlighting, which my preferred shell (ZSH) can do as well as you'll see soon.

ZSH is nearly fully compatible with Bash, in fact I've had to make zero changes to any existing scripts or commands to adapt. All my Bash knowledge works in ZSH.

Aside: The one area where I've personally hit differences is when writing completion scripts for programs (i.e. the menus that pop up when you hit <tab><tab>). ZSH completion scripts are pretty different (and far more functional), but I'd also wager that 99.7% of people out there are not writing
completion scripts regularly, or at all, so this should not affect them.

Since I mentioned completion scripts in the aside, programs that support ZSH completions are so much nicer, than the Bash completion variants.

Here's an example of completions of git in Bash:

Here's an example of completions of git in ZSH:

There are a bunch of ZSH features that I love, but the few that sound the smallest yet I probably use the most are case-insensitive paths, command-fix-suggestions, and not using cd to change directories.

Those sound tiny. But they add up quickly when using the terminal constantly.

In ZSH, you can type cat foo, when the file is really Foo and ZSH will auto-correct the path for you.

If you typo the command, and it's close enough to a real command, or file/directory ZSH will also suggest a fix that you can accept or ignore. For example:

$ gti commit -m "foo"
zsh: correct 'gti' to 'git' [nyae]?

Finally, instead of typing cd ../blah/ you can just type ../blah/.

Even though there is so much more ZSH can do, let's just assume you're sold, how do we install and use it? Here's my setup:

$ sudo dnf install zsh
  (install ZSH)

$ sudo chsh -s $(which zsh) $USER
  (change my default shell to ZSH)

Note: chsh is no longer available by default in some distributions. In Fedora, it's included in the package util-linux-user.

Then I use the following ~/.zshrc configuration:

ENABLE_CORRECTION="true"
COMPLETION_WAITING_DOTS="true" export PATH=/usr/local/bin:$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH
export SSH_KEY_PATH="~/.ssh/rsa_id"
export TERM="xterm-256color"
fpath+=~/.zfunc
compinit -U

Oh-My-ZSH

ZSH by itself is pretty plain, even if functional. There is a ZSH framework that
adds a ton of useful functionality called oh-my-zsh. It also supports things like themeing your prompt and whatnot but I don't really use that part as I rely

on other prompt software.

To install, you clone the repository and add a few lines to your ~/.zshrc:

$ git clone https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh ~/.oh-my-zsh

Then add a few lines to the top your ZSH configuration file:

export ZSH=$HOME/.oh-my-zsh
ZSH_THEME="robbyrussell"
plugins=(git systemd)
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh

That ZSH_THEME variable controls the theme, and there are a ton. However, in the following steps I disable it in favor of other options.

The plugins is the next most important line. There are a ton of plugins, and the ones listed above just a few that apply to almost any box. In the following sections we'll add a few more. Many plugins simply add the shell completions for particular commands, while others add entirely new functionality. It's
definitely worth a look through the list of plugins to see if any catch your eye for software you use frequently.

NOTE
There is a small startup cost associated with plugins, so it's good to only add the ones you'll use frequently.

Auto-Suggestions

This is the feature that most people love from Fish. It uses your command history to suggest commands live as you type, and frankly I love it too.

To add this functionality to ZSH you clone a repo, and add a plugin.

$ git clone https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-autosuggestions ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-~/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/plugins/zsh-autosuggestions

Then add zsh-autosuggestions to your plugins array in your ~/.zshrc

Syntax Highlighting

Another feature people love from Fish. This one colors the command you're typing green if the command is found and valid, or red if not. This again sounds so small, but can be such a huge help when typing commands you don't use often or
when copy/pasting from other medium. It can even catch typos before running the command. I love it.

Again, a simple clone and add a plugin.

$ git clone https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-syntax-highlighting.git ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-~/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/plugins/zsh-syntax-highlighting

Then add zsh-syntax-highlighting to the plugins array.

Abbrev-Alias

This plugin expands aliases automatically. For example, I have an alias of gl='git log --graph --all --oneline --decorate'. Using this plugin, when I type gl followed by <space> (assuming I want to add on to the command) or
<enter> to run the command the gl is replaced by the real underlying command.

Why would you want this?! Because all too often people memorize the alias, but not the command it represents. This means when you SSH into a server, or on a machine without all your custom aliases you can feel at a disadvantage. Using
abbrev-alias keeps these real commands front and center so that one at least sees them as frequently as they're run.

To install, you clone a repo and source the script in your ~/.zshrc, and use abbrev-alias instead of the normal alias command.

$ git clone https://github.com/momo-lab/zsh-abbrev-alias ~/.config/zsh-abbrev-alias/
$ echo 'source $HOME/.config/zsh-abbrev-alias/abbrev-alias.plugin.zsh' >> ~/.zshrc

And then for example, to add the gl alias I mentioned earlier

abbrev-alias gl='git log --graph --all --oneline --decorate'

Liberal use of aliases is a key aspect of quickly using the terminal. This plugin removes the biggest detriment of why some people dislike aliases!

NOTE:
The only time I still use normal aliases is if I'm replacing a command with another, i.e. alias ls=exa and the flags/options on the two commands are compatible (or nearly so).

Sudo

Another tiny quality of life improvement is the ability to quickly add sudo to the beginning of any command. If I'm half-way through typing a long command and realize I forgot to add sudo, in Bash I may have to erase the command and
start over, or move the cursor to the beginning of the line to type sudo and back to the end, or if the command is safe to run as an unprivileged user just run it and then quickly run sudo !! to replay the command. However, all
of those are not great and can be slow.

This plugin allows me to simply type <esc><esc> which adds sudo and keeps my cursor wherever I had it. As a bonus, it doesn't even negatively affect vi-mode (which we'll see next!) which also uses <esc>.

To install simply add sudo to the plugins array as it's built in to oh-my-zsh.

Vi Mode

This plugin is only useful if you're used to Vi(m) keybindings. I use it constantly. When combined with starship below, this even better because you get a visual indicator on your prompt if you're in Normal mode or Insert mode.

To install, simply add vi-mode to your plugins array as it's built in to oh-my-zsh.

Custom Prompts

Custom prompts allow you to visually determine facts about you're environment without having to run additional commands. Most of the time, all these bits of information you can find via manual commands, but to efficiently use the terminal we try to minimize the manual commands we have to use.

Some people prefer this information in other locations like a tmux status line. However, I prefer to have most of the information about my ephemeral environment in my shell prompt.

'Ephemeral environment' means it can change from command to command, or directory to directory. Non-ephemeral information is items like machine resource information, tickers, clocks, etc. Non-ephemeral information I prefer in places like tmux or my desktop environment (DE), not my prompt.

To combat the problem where long paths, or lots of bits of information can start to take up a large portion of the current line, I prefer prompt solutions which allow multi-line prompts where all the "status info" is on the first line, and my cursor/command is on the second line.

There are two great solutions to prompts; Starship and Powerlevel10k (based on Powerlevel9k). I find Powerlevel10k to be faster, but Starship is easier to customize, has some great features, and is easier to install. I use Starship by
default, but have switched to Powerlevel10k from time to time.

Starship

The starship prompt is run with a single binary, and configured with a single file which is one reason I really like it.

The demo site has a great GIF showing all the features and the why. As well as how to configure all the various items. I have a customized prompt, but the default is perfectly acceptable as well.

To install:

$ curl -fsSL https://starship.rs/install.sh | bash

To use, simply comment out your ZSH_THEME variable, and add this line to your ~/.zshrc (which the install script may have already added):

eval "$(starship init zsh)"

Powerlevel10k

To install we clone a repo, set our ZSH_THEME and configure a ~/.p10k file. I find it less configurable when it comes to the actual items I want displayed, but I also find it faster than starship at times since it was designed with ZSH
in mind (whereas starship is designed to be used with any shell).

Clone the project:

$ git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k.git ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-$HOME/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/themes/powerlevel10k

Then we set our ZSH_THEME="powerlevel10k/powerlevel10k".

When we open the terminal again we'll be prompted to configure a .p10k file interactively.

Tools

Now that we've set up our shell and prompt, there are a few tools I use constantly while in the terminal. Some are related to the actual shell, while others are just general use tools I find well worth the install.

Terminator, Tilix, or tmux

To begin, I'll mention a bit a cheat. The actual terminal emulator itself. There is one area where the terminal emulator itself can greatly improve one's efficiency.

Tiling.

Both Terminator and Tilix provide "tiling" to allow one to quickly open up another terminal to the side or below the current terminal. Terminator also allows one to define "groups" and type in one terminal, but broadcast each
command to all terminals.

This can be a huge productivity boost over disjoint terminals that require one to constantly move the mouse back and forth between windows, etc.

The alternative to this is if you're using a true tiling window manager such as i3, or tmux to handle the tiling inside a single terminal session.

NOTE:
Of course you could use tmux inside Tilix, inside i3...I feel like there is an Xzibit joke there.

Many people find using Terminator or Tilix to be a good introduction to tiling that provides a good mix between usability and functionality without fully committing to something like tmux or a Tiling Window Manager.

I'd don't personally use Tilix or Terminator much, as i3 handles all the tiling for me. I do, however, use tmux when SSH'ed into various servers and want to tile panes without having to re-authenticate.

FZF

The fuzzy finder. This tool is so amazing that it deserves a full post itself.

It fuzzy matches through lists and allows one to interactively select one (or multiple) items. Sounds simple.

When it comes to ZSH and the like the two features I use constantly are ctrl-r to fuzzy search through my history and ctrl-t to search through file paths.

Most package managers include the fzf package, and place the appropriate configuration files. All you have to do is install it, and source the ZSH script in your ~/.zshrc

$ sudo dnf install fzf
$ echo '[ -f ~/.fzf.zsh ] && source ~/.fzf.zsh' >> ~/.zshrc

FZF can also be used anywhere a list is produced and you want to pick an (or multiple) elements.

ctrl-r

Pressing <ctrl>-r will bring up a list of commands which you can fuzzy match through, press <ctrl>-up/down (or <ctrl>-j/k for vim keybindings) and hit <enter> on a selection to have that command pasted into your command line.

It's S-O-O-O much better than pressing up two hundred times to find the command you want in your history.

ctrl-t

This works the same as <ctrl>-r, except its a list of paths. 'Nuf said.

Navi

navi is about fuzzy search-able command cheat sheets. Yes please.

With this tool, I can search through (and create my own) command cheat sheets. It's difficult to explain, but when I press <ctrl>+n a TUI pops up (also using fzf by the way) which I can fuzzy match through. Upon selecting a command,
that command can have templates where I fill in the required information. Once complete, I'm dropped back to a terminal with the filled out complete command. Amazing!

Here's a GIF that explains it better than words can. I'm adding a new port the firewall via firewalld:

I tend to make more generic commands, and then will tweak the invocation manually at the end (which with vi-mode is super fast).

Installation is done by either building from source, or using bash:

$ bash <(curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/denisidoro/navi/master/scripts/install)

Making your own cheat sheets is beyond the scope of this post, but highly worth it. It's also possible to use other's repositories and have them auto updated. You can find my own sheets in a GitHub repository

By default, navi can be run from the command line normally. Setting up the <ctrl>-n keybinding can be accomplished by eval'ing the built-in widget command in your ~/.zshrc:

eval "$(navi widget zsh)"

By default the keybinding is <ctrl>-g. I prefer to just run navi widget zsh and paste the output into my ~/.zshrc and change the keybinding from g to n, and also point it to my own cheat repositories locally.

Conclusion

That's all for now. I could write more (and perhaps will later) about some of the tools I use regularly in the command line, such as exa, Ripgrep, fd-find, or bat. There's also the potential to write about the aesthetic changes I make
to improve my enjoyment of the terminal, such as font selection or which terminal emulator I actually use.

Mehreen Mallick Fiona - Nov 14

Leonardo Montini - Nov 22

item.title: 什么是区块链？ | What is a Blockchain?
item.description:
区块链实际上是一连串的区块。

把它想象成一块一块地排列在一起的块，每个块都是独一无二的，是前一个块的延续。

现在你可能想知道，这些块里面有什么？ 你可以从字面上把这些块看作存储数据的存储单元，这样我们就可以在这些块中存储不同种类的东西。

您可能听说过计算机中的数据存储，数据存储就是计算机中保存的数据。 区块链是一种开放的、去中心化的数据存储方式。 区块链中的数据存储在分布在许多计算机网络中的块链中，而不是存储在一台计算机上。 因为这个数据存储是开放和去中心化的，任何人都可以随时访问、读取和存储其中的信息。

每个区块链都有一个共识机制，这种分布式网络就写入该数据存储的新信息达成一致的方式，确保数据是真实的，因此是值得信赖的。

比特币白皮书中指出，双重支出永远不应该发生。 这是由于比特币分散式分类帐，连接到网络的每个节点都确保没有硬币被双花。

与普通数据存储不同，存储在区块链中的数据是不可变的，因此一旦数据进入其中一个区块，就无法更改。 参与网络的每个人（或大多数人）都会得到这些数据的副本，因此如果一个人试图改变他们的数据，就会与其他人（大多数人）发生冲突，他们可能会被踢出网络。

在区块链中，单点故障也是不可能的。 例如，在比特币区块链中，有数千个节点（服务器）连接到网络。 如果某个特定节点宕机，网络不会宕机，其他节点将继续运行。

一旦暂停，forwardschool 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被取消。

解除暂停后，forwardschool 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，forwardschool 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 forwardschool 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

原文:

A blockchain is literally a chain of blocks.

Think of it as blocks lined together piece by piece and each block is unique and a continuation of the previous.

Now you might be wondering, what is inside these blocks? You can literally think of these blocks as storage units that stores data so we can store different kinds of things in these blocks.

You may have heard of data storage in Computers, a data storage is just data held in a computer. A blockchain is an open and decentralized data storage. Data in a blockchain are stored in chains of blocks spread across many computers network, instead of on a single computer. Because this data storage is open and decentralized, anyone can access, read, and store information in it at any time.

Each blockchain has a consensus mechanism, a way in which this distributed network agrees on what new information is written on that data storage, ensuring that the data is true and therefore trustworthy.

It is stated in the bitcoin whitepaper, a double spend should never happen. This is due to the bitcoin decentralized ledger, every node connected to the network ensures that no coins have been double spent.

Unlike a normal data storage, data stored in a blockchain are immutable, so once a data get into one of these blocks, it can't be changed. Everyone(or majority) participating in the network gets a copy of these data, so if an individual try to change theirs, there will be a conflict with the rest(majority) and they may get kicked out of the network.

In blockchain, a single point of failure is also not possible. In the bitcoin blockchain for example, there are thousands of nodes(severs) connected to the network. If a particular node goes down, the network does not go down, other nodes will continue to run.

Hello Coders!

This article presents Pixel Boostrap 5, an open-source and actively supported theme for Django. Using this library, available as a PyPi Package, any Django developer can code pixel-perfect websites in minutes. UI is super easy to extend and the configuration is minimal. Thank You!

Pixel Lite is a free and open-source Bootstrap 5 user interface kit featuring over 80 fully coded UI elements and example pages that will help you prototype and build a website for your next project.

This open-source library implements all pages required by Django.contrib.AUTH, the default authentication system for Django plus a registration page.

Product Highlights

Pixel was built by experienced web developers with a focus on good and high-quality code.

  • ✅ Modern stack: Django & Bootstrap 5
  • ✅ Default Django authentication covered
  • ✅ All UI Kit integrated (for common users)
  • ✅ MIT License
  • ✅ Free Support via Email & Discord

Pixel Lite is an extension of the Bootstrap 5 CSS framework with a focus on heavy styling customization but also with components that are not found by default in the framework, such as new cards, date pickers, timeline items, and example pages.

Django Theme Pixel - About Page

item.description:
西德尼亚历克斯

发表于喂！ 没有帖子 passado expliquei como criar REST API 简单实用程序 o Nitro eo Prisma combinado 和 MongoDB。
